text:
King's College Hospital

Department of Neurosciences

Clinic Date: 27 May 2014

Dr Alex Morgan
Riverside Medical Centre
12 Brook Street, London W1F 8AA

Dear Dr Alex Morgan,

Daniel Harris, DOB: 14-03-1982, Hospital No: H762309 NHS No. 9475206831
28 Willow Court, Hammersmith, London W6 7PT

Thank you for asking me to review your patient. He attended today with his partner and provided additional history concerning recent events, including a seizure that occurred during air travel last month. He described significant anticipatory anxiety associated with flying and busy transport hubs, and he feels this may have contributed to the in‑flight episode. There were no injuries, and cabin staff supported a calm, low‑stimulus environment until he recovered.

Regarding his current pattern, he reports variable spells with loss of awareness, occasional rocking movements, and semi-purposeful vocalisation, followed by protracted fatigue and confusion. His seizures typically occur in clusters, generally spaced 2 to 4 days apart, though brief periods of daily seizures have been reported. VNS is used more frequently, and Buccolam was administered once each month over the past four months. He and his partner keep a diary and note that disrupted sleep, travel-related stress, and missed meals appear to precede some events. No consistent nocturnal predominance is identified, and there is no clear lateralising semiology volunteered. He denies recent head injury. There is no new focal neurological symptom between events.

He is currently taking levetiracetam and lacosamide as baseline therapy. He uses Buccolam as a rescue option as above. He has a VNS in situ; device checks in the community were last done six months ago according to his record card. He has not had prolonged status episodes requiring A&E attendance this year.

On examination today he was alert, oriented, and cooperative. Speech, cranial nerves, tone, power, coordination, and gait were unremarkable. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations were normal. There were no features of intercurrent infection. Mood appeared mildly anxious when discussing upcoming work travel, but there was no evidence of thought disorder or psychotic features.

Given the variable semiology and the lack of a unifying pattern on history alone, I cannot ascribe a specific epilepsy classification at this stage. We discussed further characterisation. I have arranged: (1) ambulatory EEG with home video capture to sample typical events; (2) VNS interrogation and parameter review; and (3) updated bloods including antiseizure medication levels, FBC, U&E, LFTs, and vitamin D. I also suggested a structured seizure action plan for travel days, including ensuring adequate sleep, hydration, and pre-arranged seating, with his partner familiar with rescue steps.

We acknowledged his travel anxiety. He is agreeable to brief psychoeducation around anxiety management strategies and will self-refer to local IAPT services; he declined formal psychology referral today but will reconsider after the EEG results. Occupationally, he remains in administrative work and is avoiding lone business travel pending further assessment. He is aware of standard safety advice including swimming with supervision, ladder/height avoidance, and safe bathing practices.

I will review him after the ambulatory EEG and VNS check, or sooner if there is any escalation in frequency, prolonged events, or injuries. Please let me know if there are concerns in the interim.

Yours sincerely,

entities.text: His seizures typically occur in clusters, generally spaced 2 to 4 days apart, though brief periods of daily seizures have been reported. VNS is used more frequently, and Buccolam was administered once each month over the past four months

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit frequency statements in the letter's description of the current pattern. The key sentence says seizures "typically occur in clusters, generally spaced 2 to 4 days apart." This provides a clear numeric interval for how often events occur. The mention of "brief periods of daily seizures" indicates occasional short-lived increases, not the sustained or typical rate. Buccolam use "once each month" reflects rescue medication usage, not baseline seizure frequency. The letter does not quantify seizures per cluster, so the cluster-specific format cannot be completed. Therefore, normalise the dominant stated interval to the allowed format as 1 per 2 to 4 day.
reference: His seizures typically occur in clusters, generally spaced 2 to 4 days apart, though brief periods of daily seizures have been reported. VNS is used more frequently, and Buccolam was administered once each month over the past four months
